source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 8.5 File naming convention (pp. 146-150)

questions.q: Which of these file-naming facts is correct in Linux?
options: Names are up to 255 characters, all ASCII characters are allowed, and case is significant
Names are limited to 8 characters and are case-insensitive
File extensions are mandatory
Names may not contain lowercase letters
questions.why: Names are coded on 255 characters, any ASCII is allowed, upper/lowercase differ, and most files need no extension.

questions.q: What is the effect of a leading dot (.) in a file name?
options: It hides the file from a simple ls (you need ls -a to see it)
It makes the file executable
It marks the file as a directory
It compresses the file
questions.why: A name beginning with '.' is only hidden from a plain ls; ls -a reveals it.

questions.q: In an 'ls -l' listing, what does the very first character of the 10-character block indicate?
options: The file type ('-' for an ordinary file, 'd' for a directory, ...)
The owner's first initial
The read permission for the owner
The number of hard links
questions.why: The first character is the file type; the remaining nine are the permissions (3x3 for user/group/others).

questions.q: Which set correctly lists Linux's seven file types?
options: - ordinary, d directory, b block device, c character device, s socket, p pipe, l link
- ordinary, d directory, x executable, r readable, w writable, s system, t temp
f file, d directory, l link, m mount, n node, p partition, s swap
a archive, b binary, c code, d data, e exec, f file, g group
questions.why: The seven types are -, d, b, c, s, p and l.

questions.q: What is the difference between a 'b' and a 'c' special file?
options: 'b' is a block device (disks, USB drives); 'c' is a character device (serial interfaces like mouse, keyboard)
'b' is a character device; 'c' is a block device
'b' is a binary; 'c' is C source
Both are the same kind of device file
questions.why: Block ('b') devices are things like disks; character ('c') devices are serial-interface devices such as keyboards and mice.

options: Passing information between processes on a First In, First Out basis
Storing compressed archives
Linking two disks together
Holding the boot loader
questions.why: A pipe (FIFO) file passes transient data between processes: one writes, another reads, and the data is then gone.

options: Bidirectional inter-process communication, on local or remote systems
One-way communication only
Storage of user passwords
Mounting of removable media
questions.why: Socket files enable two-way inter-process communication (local or networked) and use a filesystem inode.
